title: AVM Paul Masiyer (Rtd.) Declares Intention to Represent Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency in 2027
excerpt: Decorated Air Vice Marshal and Plateau State son announces his bid for the House of Representatives, pledging to bring 35 years of military discipline and strategic leadership to the constituency's deepest challenges.
body:
Plateau State, Nigeria — Air Vice Marshal Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.), a son of Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency, has officially declared his intention to contest the House of Representatives seat for the constituency in the 2027 general elections under the All Progressives Congress (APC).

AVM Masiyer, who retired from the Nigerian Air Force after a career spanning more than three decades — including senior appointments as Chief of Logistics at Headquarters Nigerian Air Force and Commandant of the Military Training Centre — framed his declaration as a direct response to the persistent challenges facing the people of Mangu and Bokkos: unresolved insecurity, agricultural disruption, inadequate healthcare, and years of federal underrepresentation.

"For too long, our people have endured suffering that could have been mitigated with stronger, more experienced representation at the federal level. I have spent my career managing national security and logistics at the highest levels. I know how government works from the inside. I am returning home to put that knowledge in service of Mangu/Bokkos," AVM Masiyer said at his declaration ceremony.

His campaign is anchored on eight pillars: security and lasting peace, agricultural and economic support, education, healthcare, youth empowerment, infrastructure, cultural heritage, and national representation. AVM Masiyer called on all stakeholders across Mangu and Bokkos — traditional leaders, youth groups, women's networks, and farming communities — to unite behind the movement for experienced and accountable federal representation.

title: AVM Masiyer Calls for Urgent Federal Intervention After Christmas Eve Attacks Devastate Bokkos and Mangu
excerpt: Following coordinated attacks on Christmas Eve 2023 that killed hundreds across Bokkos, Barkin Ladi, and Mangu LGAs, AVM Paul Masiyer demands a permanent federal security presence — not reactive condemnations.
body:
Plateau State, Nigeria — The Christmas Eve attacks of 2023, which swept through over 20 communities across Bokkos, Barkin Ladi, and Mangu Local Government Areas, killing an estimated 195 people in the initial assault and displacing more than 18,000, marked one of the most devastating episodes of violence in recent memory for Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency. Health authorities in Plateau State later confirmed the cumulative death toll at 865, including 160 children, as attacks continued through January and February 2024.

According to the United Nations and humanitarian agencies responding in the aftermath, more than 160 villages were affected — 44 in Bokkos and 109 in Mangu LGA. Over 409 houses were burnt, hundreds of families fled to IDP camps in Jos, and farming families from Jing and Bungha communities in Mangu, and Mbar in Bokkos, described midnight attacks that forced them to flee with nothing.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) responded with a call for systemic change. "Condemnation is not a security strategy. What the people of Bokkos and Mangu need is a permanent, well-equipped security presence in these flashpoint areas — not forces that arrive after the killing is done. The federal government must treat this constituency as a region to be protected, not merely pitied."

AVM Masiyer, whose military career included senior responsibility for the operational readiness of the entire Nigerian Air Force, argued that the constituency's crisis demands federal-level resources, deployments, and accountability mechanisms that only a credible, experienced representative can secure. "This is precisely why experienced representation matters. I know the security architecture of this country from the inside. Mangu/Bokkos deserves a voice in Abuja that understands what actionable security looks like."

title: Mangu's Food Basket Crisis: How Insecurity Is Threatening Plateau's Irish Potato Supply and Nigeria's Food Security
excerpt: With 96 farming communities in Mangu LGA affected by violence and Irish potato cultivation at 20 per cent of normal output, the crisis in Mangu/Bokkos has national food security implications that demand federal attention.
body:
Mangu, Plateau State, Nigeria — Long recognised as the food basket of Plateau State, Mangu Local Government Area hosts the largest commercial maize market in the state and is among Nigeria's most productive zones for Irish potato cultivation. The sustained insecurity following the December 2023 attacks has placed this agricultural identity under severe strain.

Sohotden Mathias Ibrahim, director of culture at the Mwaghavul Development Association and director of the IDP camp in Mangu, confirmed that Irish potato cultivation in Mangu had dropped to as low as 20 per cent of normal output. Of 96 farming communities in Mangu affected by the crisis, 56 were so badly impacted that farmers could not cultivate at all during the dry season. Farmers like Christopher Rafan of Bokkos described watching crops they could not harvest rot in fields they could not safely access. Moses Saya Loms, a traditional titleholder in Bokkos, had planted 20 bags of Irish potato seedlings in November and estimated losses of over 60 bags of expected yield at an average seedling cost of N20,000 per bag.

Financial analysts noted the national implications: Mangu's maize market directly influences the cost of poultry, spaghetti, and other staples across Nigeria. AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) has consistently framed the constituency's agricultural crisis as inseparable from its security crisis. "Our farmers are not just feeding Mangu and Bokkos. They are feeding Nigeria. When they are displaced and their crops rot in unguarded fields, that is a national emergency — and it requires national-level intervention of the kind only a credible federal representative can secure."

AVM Masiyer's agricultural agenda includes advocacy for federal emergency farm support, market access road rehabilitation, post-harvest storage infrastructure, and direct farmer linkage to federal financing programmes — all conditional on first establishing the security conditions that allow farming communities to safely return to their land.

title: Operation Safe Haven Convenes Bokkos and Mangu Stakeholders in Jos — AVM Masiyer Supports Community-Integrated Security
excerpt: Following a resurgence of attacks, Operation Safe Haven met with Bokkos and Mangu stakeholders at 3 Division Headquarters, Jos — a development AVM Paul Masiyer described as necessary but insufficient without structural commitment.
body:
Jos, Plateau State, Nigeria — The Special Task Force Operation Safe Haven convened a meeting with key stakeholders from Bokkos and Mangu local government areas at Headquarters 3 Division Nigerian Army in Jos on 24 April 2024, following a resurgence of attacks in both areas. Commander Major General AE Abubakar called on stakeholders to support resettlement efforts and facilitate the return of displaced persons to their ancestral homes.

The meeting reflected growing acknowledgement within the security establishment that kinetic measures alone have not been sufficient to address the root causes of violence in Mangu/Bokkos. The United States Institute of Peace, which has supported peacebuilding facilitators operating in Mangu, noted that reactive, force-based interventions have repeatedly proven ineffective and that sustainable peace requires locally led, community-integrated approaches — including structured dialogue between the Mwaghavul farming communities and Fulani pastoral communities that have been central to the conflict.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) welcomed the consultation but argued it must be accompanied by structural commitments. "A meeting is a beginning, not a solution. What our communities need is a permanent, operational security presence with real accountability — not periodic engagements followed by a return to the same cycle of attack and condemnation. Our community leaders, traditional rulers, and local intelligence networks must be built into the security architecture, not consulted and then forgotten."

AVM Masiyer, drawing on his senior military command experience, has proposed a constituency-level security engagement framework that integrates traditional institutions, community early warning systems, and formal security structures into a coordinated, accountable response architecture specifically designed for the realities of Mangu and Bokkos.

title: 65,000 Displaced: The Hidden Humanitarian Crisis in Mangu and Bokkos That Demands Federal Action
excerpt: Amnesty International documents that attacks across Plateau State have internally displaced 65,000 people, with Mangu and Bokkos among the worst-affected areas — communities emptied, IDP camps overwhelmed, farming livelihoods destroyed.
body:
Plateau State, Nigeria — Over the course of two years, armed attacks across 167 rural communities in Plateau State — with Mangu and Bokkos among the most severely affected — resulted in the internal displacement of an estimated 65,000 people, according to documentation by Amnesty International. Some communities in the constituency were displaced more than once after IDP camps were themselves targeted in subsequent attacks.

The scale of the crisis is reflected not only in numbers but in lived realities: farming families who cannot access their fields; women who gave birth in school compounds after fleeing midnight attacks on communities like Jing in Mangu LGA; men who returned to find villages emptied and crops destroyed. The majority of those displaced are smallholder farmers — the backbone of Plateau's agricultural economy — whose removal from the land has compounded both personal poverty and regional food insecurity.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) described the displacement crisis as one of the defining failures of federal representation in Mangu/Bokkos. "When 65,000 of our people are driven from their homes and the National Assembly remains largely silent, that is a representation failure. When IDP camps in Bokkos and Mangu are attacked — again — and there is no credible federal response, that is a representation failure. This constituency needs someone at the National Assembly who will not just raise the matter but force it onto the legislative agenda."

AVM Masiyer committed to championing the rights and return of displaced persons from Mangu/Bokkos as a legislative priority — including advocating for a dedicated federal resettlement fund, accountability mechanisms for security agencies, and the integration of community-based protection structures into formal security frameworks.

title: Five Bokkos Communities Attacked in One Week — AVM Masiyer Urges Legislative Emergency Response
excerpt: Between 27 March and 3 April 2025, coordinated attacks struck Daffo, Gwande, Hurti, Manguna, and Ruwi in Bokkos LGA — AVM Paul Masiyer calls for the House of Representatives to declare a security emergency in the constituency.
body:
Bokkos, Plateau State, Nigeria — Between 27 March and 3 April 2025, coordinated attacks struck five communities — Daffo, Gwande, Hurti, Manguna, and Ruwi — across Bokkos Local Government Area in a single week. The 3 April attack was described by humanitarian observers as particularly severe, with entire families killed and community infrastructure including bore holes, clinics, and schools deliberately destroyed, according to Amnesty International documentation.

The attacks followed a pattern documented across 167 rural communities in Plateau State over two years: coordinated, overnight, targeting civilians and community infrastructure, with delayed or absent security response. Amnesty International confirmed that armed attacks in Plateau State numbered 38 in the period covered by their most recent major report, with Bokkos, Mangu, and other constituency LGAs consistently among the most affected.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) called for legislative emergency designation. "What is happening in Bokkos and across Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency is not a local law and order problem. It is a national security crisis that requires a national legislative response. The House of Representatives must declare this an emergency, authorise special resources for the affected communities, and create an accountability mechanism for agencies that repeatedly fail to protect our people."

AVM Masiyer called for unity among all stakeholders — traditional leaders, faith communities, youth organisations, and diaspora members — in demanding the level of federal attention the constituency deserves. "This movement is born out of the conviction that our people have suffered long enough from absent and inadequate representation. The time for a different kind of voice in Abuja has come."

title: 19 Killed in Overnight Attacks on Manja and Tangur — AVM Masiyer Calls for Community Protection Measures
excerpt: Gunmen killed six in Manja, Chakfem District of Mangu, and 13 in Tangur, Bokkos, in coordinated overnight attacks on 20 June 2025 — the latest chapter in a cycle of violence AVM Paul Masiyer says requires structural federal intervention.
body:
Mangu and Bokkos, Plateau State, Nigeria — On the night of 20 June 2025, gunmen struck two communities: six residents were killed in Manja, Chakfem District of Mangu Local Government, and 13 others lost their lives in Tangur, Bokkos County. Mathias Ibrahim, director of culture at the Mwaghavul Development Association and director of the IDP camp in Mangu, confirmed the death toll from the Mangu attack and noted that the atmosphere remained tense, with communities fearing further violence.

The Bokkos attack began at approximately 9 p.m., catching most residents unprepared. The Chakfem assault occurred earlier in the evening with the same pattern: arriving at night, firing indiscriminately, forcing entry into homes. Community leaders noted that such attacks have occurred in proximity to security installations without timely response — a pattern that has generated deep frustration and eroded trust between civilians and security forces across the constituency.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) expressed deep sorrow at the loss of life in Manja and Tangur. "Every family in Manja, in Tangur, in Chakfem, in Mushere — every community of Mangu and Bokkos that has buried its dead and watched its neighbours flee — they deserve more than condolences. They deserve a representative who will stand in the National Assembly and name these communities, demand accountability, and refuse to accept that this is simply the way things are."

AVM Masiyer reiterated his commitment to making community protection the foundational priority of his legislative agenda. "Security is not one item among eight on a list. It is the condition under which every other aspiration — farming, education, healthcare, opportunity — becomes possible. We cannot build anything on this constituency until our people are safe in their own homes."

title: 12,000 Killed Over Two Decades: Why Mangu/Bokkos Needs Different Representation at the National Assembly in 2027
excerpt: A government fact-finding committee confirmed in November 2025 that over 12,000 people have been killed in Plateau State violence across 25 years. For Mangu/Bokkos, this demands not more condemnation — but experienced, accountable legislative action.
body:
Plateau State, Nigeria — A ten-person fact-finding committee presented findings to the Plateau State Governor in November 2025 confirming that more than 12,000 people have been killed in violence across the state over two decades. The report underscored what community leaders in Mangu and Bokkos have long argued: that the frequency, scale, and systematic nature of attacks represent a crisis of governance and protection, not merely a law enforcement challenge.

Between December 2023 and February 2024 alone, over 1,300 people were killed in Plateau State — an average of more than 14 deaths per day for 90 days, with Mangu and Bokkos at the centre of the crisis. By May to November 2025, Amnesty International documented a further 2,500 deaths and 29,554 displaced persons across Mangu, Bokkos, and Barkin Ladi LGAs. The same pattern — coordinated attacks, delayed response, inadequate federal support — repeated itself with devastating regularity across 25 years.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) drew on his perspective as a career military officer with senior national security experience. "Plateau State's crisis is not a mystery. It has patterns, it has perpetrators who have largely operated without consequence. What it has lacked is the kind of persistent, knowledgeable, credible federal voice that refuses to accept business as usual. I am offering that voice to the people of Mangu/Bokkos."

AVM Masiyer committed to supporting state-level peacebuilding and resettlement efforts with complementary federal advocacy — including pushing for Mangu and Bokkos to be designated as priority federal intervention zones, demanding accountability for security lapses, and ensuring community-led peace structures receive federal recognition and funding.

title: Nine Killed in Mushere, Bokkos — Including a Church Leader: The Human Cost of Unprotected Rural Communities
excerpt: In late May 2025, nine residents of Mushere in Bokkos LGA were killed, including a local church leader — among more than 200 deaths recorded in Bokkos LGA in 2025 alone. AVM Masiyer calls for the protection of rural communities as a federal obligation.
body:
Bokkos Local Government Area, Plateau State, Nigeria — Nine people were killed in the Mushere area of Bokkos LGA in late May 2025, including a church leader whose death underscored the targeted nature of violence affecting rural communities across the constituency. The killings brought to more than 200 the number of deaths recorded in Bokkos LGA in 2025 alone, according to data compiled by the International Christian Concern.

The loss of a church leader in Mushere added a dimension of community grief extending beyond immediate families. In communities where the church serves as a central institution of social life, education, and mutual support, such losses fracture the social fabric in ways that take years to rebuild. Fear of nighttime attack has driven families across Bokkos and Mangu to abandon their homes and sleep in the bush — a reality that has persisted intermittently for years, disrupting farming, schooling, and the rhythms of community life.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) offered his condolences to the community of Mushere and called for the protection of rural communities as an urgent federal obligation. "When church leaders are killed in Mushere, when entire communities in Bokkos sleep in the bush because they cannot trust their homes will protect them, we have failed as a nation in our most basic duty to our citizens. The people of Bokkos and Mangu are not statistics. They are my people."

AVM Masiyer committed to ensuring that communities like Mushere — too often invisible in national conversations — receive the specific federal attention they deserve: targeted security deployments, humanitarian support for affected families, and accountability for perpetrators of attacks on civilian and religious communities in Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency.

title: Community Resilience in Mangu: How the Mwaghavul Development Association Has Held the Constituency Together
excerpt: While federal representation fell short, community organisations like the Mwaghavul Development Association have managed IDP camps, documented the crisis, and preserved community identity in Mangu LGA — a foundation AVM Masiyer says deserves federal recognition and support.
body:
Mangu, Plateau State, Nigeria — In the absence of sustained federal intervention, community-based organisations have been critical to maintaining cohesion and providing coordination for displaced families in Mangu/Bokkos. The Mwaghavul Development Association, whose leaders have run the IDP camp at the Pilot Science School in Mangu and documented the scale of destruction across the constituency, has provided both a cultural anchor and an essential information source for humanitarian organisations.

Mathias Ibrahim, director of culture at the Mwaghavul Development Association and director of the Mangu IDP camp, documented that 56 of 96 farming communities in Mangu LGA affected by violence were so severely impacted they could not cultivate during the dry farming season. His ongoing presence at the IDP camp and his confirmation of the death tolls during various attacks have reflected the degree to which community institutions have filled critical gaps left by inadequate federal and state response.

The Network of Nigerian Facilitators, supported by the United States Institute of Peace, has worked alongside such community leaders in Mangu — organising dialogues between Mwaghavul farming communities and Fulani pastoral communities, and facilitating direct engagement between civilian leaders and military authorities. These locally-led peacebuilding efforts have generated recommendations that have meaningfully improved relationships and communication channels in the area.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) has acknowledged the foundational role of community leaders in holding Mangu/Bokkos together. "The strength of our communities — the elders, development associations, women's groups, faith leaders — has been the only constant through years of crisis. My campaign is built on working with the structures our communities have already built. Federal support should amplify that strength, not replace it with distant condolences."

title: Who Is AVM Paul D. Masiyer? Biography of the Man Seeking to Represent Mangu/Bokkos at the National Assembly
excerpt: From the hills of Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency to the peak of the Nigerian Air Force — a profile of Air Vice Marshal Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) and the career that makes him a distinctive voice for Plateau State in 2027.
body:
Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency, Plateau State, Nigeria — Air Vice Marshal Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) was born and raised in Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency, Plateau State — the land he now seeks to represent at the National Assembly. His story is one of service: to the Nigerian Air Force, to the nation, and now to the people of his home constituency.

Over more than three decades in the Nigerian Air Force, AVM Masiyer rose to the rank of Air Vice Marshal — the peak of commissioned military service in Nigeria. His career was distinguished not only by rank but by the nature of the responsibilities he held. As Chief of Logistics at Headquarters Nigerian Air Force (HQ NAF), he was responsible for the procurement, supply chain management, and operational readiness that kept the entire Air Force mission-capable. As Commandant of the Military Training Centre, he shaped the education and professional standards of the Air Force's next generation of officers.

Retirement from the military, AVM Masiyer has consistently said, was not retirement from service. "I have spent my entire adult life in service of Nigeria. The question was never whether to continue serving — only in what form. The people of Mangu and Bokkos have endured a decade of violence, displacement, and inadequate representation. The form that service must take now is clear."

AVM Paul D. Masiyer is running under the All Progressives Congress (APC) for the Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency seat in the 2027 general elections. His eight-pillar platform has been endorsed by community leaders across both local government areas. For those searching for Paul Masiyer biography, AVM Paul D Masiyer, or AVM Paul Masiyer Plateau State, this is the record that defines his candidacy.

title: Mangu/Bokkos Youth Are Leaving — And the Constituency Cannot Afford It
excerpt: Years of insecurity and limited federal investment have driven young people from Mangu and Bokkos to Jos, Abuja, and beyond. AVM Paul Masiyer's youth empowerment agenda aims to create conditions that make staying worthwhile.
body:
Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency, Plateau State, Nigeria — The violence, displacement, and economic stagnation that have characterised life in Mangu and Bokkos have accelerated a quiet but consequential trend: young people are leaving. They leave for Jos, Abuja, Lagos, and increasingly for other countries — because the conditions that would allow them to build a future in their home constituency have not materialised.

The consequences are compounding. Farming communities weakened by insecurity lose the young men and women who might restore agricultural productivity. Schools and clinics lose the educated professionals who studied at great personal cost. The social architecture of community life — the teachers, artisans, and entrepreneurs — is depleted at the very moment it is most needed.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) has made youth retention a central element of his campaign platform. "Our young people are not leaving because they do not love this land. They are leaving because this land has not been given the investment and security it deserves. The question is not how to stop them leaving — it is how to make staying worthwhile. That requires security, skills programmes, access to federal economic opportunities, and a representative who will fight for those things every day in Abuja."

His youth agenda includes advocacy for constituency inclusion in federal skills acquisition programmes, digital training hubs in Mangu and Bokkos, apprenticeship networks connecting young people with local business owners, and a diaspora engagement channel inviting those who have left to invest in the community's recovery. AVM Masiyer is clear that youth empowerment is inseparable from the security agenda: stable communities attract investment and opportunity; insecure ones repel both.

title: State Resettlement Efforts in Bokkos and Mangu Need Federal Backing to Succeed — AVM Masiyer
excerpt: Governor Mutfwang's Operation Rainbow and resettlement committee represent important state-level commitments. AVM Paul Masiyer argues they require sustained federal legislative support to achieve lasting results for displaced communities in Mangu and Bokkos.
body:
Jos, Plateau State, Nigeria — The administration of Plateau State Governor Barrister Caleb Mutfwang has taken meaningful steps to address the humanitarian and security crisis in Bokkos, Mangu, and other affected LGAs. The revival of Operation Rainbow with an initial deployment of 600 trained community members, the establishment of a Resettlement Committee, and the inauguration of a Fact-Finding Committee in May 2025 represent genuine commitments to addressing displacement and rebuilding community safety.

The governor's personal visits to IDP camps in Mangu and Bokkos — including communities that have been displaced for nearly two decades — and his framing of the attacks as coordinated and systematic rather than spontaneous, have brought a different quality of attention to the crisis at the state level. Plans to expand Operation Rainbow to 1,450 additional recruits across all 17 LGAs, alongside procurement of vehicles and equipment for security personnel, reflect continued state-level investment in the problem.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) affirmed these state efforts while arguing that they cannot succeed without complementary federal legislative backing. "Governor Mutfwang is doing the right things at the state level, and he deserves support. But Operation Rainbow needs federal resources. Resettlement needs federal funding. The communities of Bokkos and Mangu need a member of the House of Representatives who will go to Abuja and make the case for those resources — consistently, credibly, and effectively. That is the gap I intend to fill."

AVM Masiyer committed to working in close coordination with the state government on security and resettlement — treating federal and state representation as collaborative, with the single shared objective of returning safety, dignity, and opportunity to every community in Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency.

title: AVM Paul Masiyer's Campaign Promises for Mangu/Bokkos 2027: Eight Pillars, One Purpose
excerpt: As the 2027 elections approach, AVM Paul D. Masiyer outlines the eight specific commitments that form his legislative platform for Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency — grounded in the real needs of Mangu and Bokkos communities.
body:
Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency, Plateau State, Nigeria — With the 2027 general elections approaching, AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) has set out the eight-pillar agenda that will define his legislative priorities if elected to represent Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency in the House of Representatives.

At the foundation of every pillar is security and lasting peace — because nothing else is possible without it. The insecurity that has displaced 65,000 people, destroyed crops and homes, and driven young people away from communities in Mangu and Bokkos must be addressed with a combination of federal resources, community-integrated protection structures, accountability for security agencies, and sustained legislative pressure.

Building on that foundation, AVM Masiyer's agenda covers: federal support for farmers and the agricultural economy of Mangu/Bokkos; improved schools and functional healthcare in every ward; youth empowerment and skills programmes that give young people a reason to stay; roads, utilities, and infrastructure investment; targeted support for women, the elderly, and the most vulnerable; preservation of the rich cultural heritage of the Mwaghavul, Challa, and all peoples of the constituency; and active, credible representation ensuring Mangu/Bokkos is heard when consequential federal decisions are made.

"These are not campaign promises designed to be forgotten after election day. They are the commitments I will be held to — by my community, by this movement, and by my own conscience," AVM Masiyer said. Voters searching for AVM Paul Masiyer campaign promises, Mangu Bokkos development plan, or Mangu Bokkos candidate 2027 will find in this platform a clear, specific, and accountable programme.

title: How to Support AVM Paul Masiyer and Join the PMM Movement for Mangu/Bokkos 2027
excerpt: The AVM Paul Masiyer Movement is a grassroots campaign built on people, not just politics. Here is how you can be part of the movement to bring experienced, accountable representation to Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency.
body:
Mangu/Bokkos Federal Constituency, Plateau State, Nigeria — The AVM Paul Masiyer Movement — PMM 2027 — is built on the conviction that the people of Mangu and Bokkos deserve representation that is experienced, credible, and genuinely committed to their wellbeing. It is organised around ward-level volunteer networks across both local government areas, a WhatsApp cascade system that moves information from campaign headquarters to individual communities in real time, and a diaspora channel connecting the sons and daughters of the constituency wherever they are.

The PMM Women's Network has been active across Mangu and Bokkos, and the PMM Farmers' Network is building direct connections between the campaign and agricultural communities most affected by the constituency's insecurity and economic challenges. Ward coordinators in communities across both LGAs have already been briefed and activated.

Supporting AVM Paul Masiyer and the PMM can take many forms: registering as a ward volunteer, sharing campaign content across WhatsApp and social media, creating a personalised PMM support poster through the campaign website, organising or attending community meetings in Mangu or Bokkos, or contributing as part of the diaspora network. Every person who joins the movement signals that Mangu/Bokkos is ready for a different kind of representation.

"This movement belongs to the people of Mangu and Bokkos. I am the candidate, but the movement is yours," AVM Masiyer said. To support Paul Masiyer, join the PMM movement, or volunteer for the Mangu/Bokkos 2027 campaign, visit the campaign website or connect on WhatsApp at +234 707 060 9297.

title: Angwan Rukuba Palm Sunday Attack: AVM Masiyer Mourns with Plateau State, Demands Action
excerpt: On Palm Sunday, 29 March 2026, gunmen killed at least 27 civilians in Angwan Rukuba, Jos North LGA. AVM Paul Masiyer was present in Jos for President Tinubu's condolence visit on 2 April — and spoke directly from what he witnessed, demanding structural action and invoking security as the foundation of his platform.
body:
Jos, Plateau State, Nigeria — On the evening of Palm Sunday, 29 March 2026, gunmen stormed the Gari Ya Waye area of Angwan Rukuba in Jos North Local Government Area, opening fire on civilians returning from church services. At least 27 people were killed and dozens more were injured in one of the most shocking attacks on worshippers in recent memory.

The Plateau State Government imposed a 48-hour curfew across Jos North in response. Governor Caleb Mutfwang condemned the attack as "barbaric and unprovoked" and visited the affected community to commiserate with bereaved families.

President Bola Tinubu travelled to Jos on 2 April 2026 to offer presidential condolences, directed security agencies to pursue the perpetrators without delay, and announced the deployment of 5,000 AI-enabled CCTV cameras across Plateau State as part of a broader security response.

AVM Paul D. Masiyer (Rtd.) was present in Jos during the presidential condolence visit — joining fellow Plateau sons and daughters in standing with a grieving community. Speaking from what he witnessed firsthand, AVM Masiyer issued the following statement:

"I was there. I stood in Jos and I looked into the faces of people who had just buried their loved ones — people who went to church on Palm Sunday and never came home. That image does not leave you. Plateau State is our home, and what happened in Angwan Rukuba is not a Jos problem — it is a Plateau problem, and it is a Nigeria problem. We cannot keep gathering to condole and then returning to business as usual. Security is the first of my eight pillars — not because it sounds good on a campaign poster, but because I have spent my career understanding what it takes to protect people, and I know that what our communities are getting is not enough. I will not stop saying that until something changes."

AVM Masiyer's presence at the Jos condolence gathering — and his public statement in its aftermath — reflects the commitment at the heart of his 2027 candidacy: that Mangu/Bokkos and all of Plateau State need a federal voice willing to stand in the middle of grief, speak plainly about failure, and demand the structural change that prevents the next attack.
